__NOTOC__ {{Chess Opening Theory/Position |King's Pawn opening |eco=[[Chess/ECOB|B00–B99]], [[Chess/ECOC|C00–99]] |parent=[[../|Starting position]] |responses=
* [[/1...c5|1...c5 - Sicilian Defence]] * [[/1...e5|1...e5 - Open Game]] * [[/1...e6|1...e6 - French Defence]] * [[/1...c6|1...c6 - Caro-Kann Defence]] * [[/1...d5|1...d5 - Scandinavian Defence]] * [[/1...d6|1...d6 - Pirc Defence]] * [[/1...g6|1...g6 - Modern Defence]] }} == 1. e4 · King's Pawn opening ==
Best by test.
— Bobby Fischer{{cite book |last=Fischer |first=Bobby |author-link=Wikipedia:Bobby Fischer |year=1969 |title=My 60 Memorable Games |url=https://archive.org/details/my60memorablegam00fisc |url-access=registration |chapter=45. Fischer–Bisguier, New York State Open 1963 |publisher=Simon and Schuster |isbn=978-0-671-21483-8 |page=[https://archive.org/details/my60memorablegam00fisc/page/280 280]}}
1. e4, the '''King's Pawn Opening''', is the most popular first move at all levels of the game. 1. e4 opens lines to develop the queen and the king's bishop. It also fights for control of the centre. 1. e4 directly controls d5 and f5, which may or may not be relevant later because Black, if they wanted to, could just take those squares away again by playing 1...e6. If White wants to develop some pieces, the most important square they control by playing 1. e4 is, in fact, the e4 square itself: as long as there's a white pawn on e4, there can't be a black pawn on e4, which means White's g1-knight can be developed to f3 without fear of getting kicked away by a pawn and losing a tempo. Black has several ways to respond. The main idea is to find a way to prevent White from achieving or maintaining a two-pawn centre with both e4 and d4. They may try to: * Control the d4 square. * Attack White's e4 pawn directly. * Prepare to establish their own pawn on d5. * Let White build a centre but prepare to undermine it later. === Control d4 === If Black can control d4 with a pawn, then it will be hard for White to establish pawns on both d4 and e4 and keep them there. Black has two pawn pushes that control the d4 square: [[/1...e5|'''1...e5''']] the Open game, and [[/1...c5|'''1...c5''']] the Sicilian defence. It is no accident that these are the most popular continuations at all levels.1...c5 and 1...e5 comprise 46% and 23% of continuations in the Lichess Master database, and 19% and 45% in the Lichess database. [[/1...e5|'''1...e5''']] lets Black take space in the centre. It is the classical reply: traditionally 1. e4 openings are divided into 1...e5, the Open game, and the rest, the Semi-Open games. Now if 2. d4, Black can take it and prevent White from controlling all that central space. Just as 1. e4 helps White to indirectly control the e4 square, 1...e5 prevents White from playing 2. e5, and so ensures Black can develop their knight to f6. White's argument is that a symmetrical position is eventually going to favour the player moving first. White's usual continuation is 2. Nf3, a threat to eliminate the e5 pawn so that they may play d4 securely. [[/1...c5|'''1...c5''']] is the mainline in master-level games today. If White plays d4, Black can take it with the flank c pawn, keeping their two more important central pawns. An asymmetric opening, it is thought of as more combative than 1...e5. {{clear}}
{{Chess/board|moves=1. e4 e5|caption=Open game}} {{Chess/board|moves=1. e4 c5|caption=Sicilian defence}}
=== Attack e4 === Black can confront White's e-pawn immediately to open up the centre. [[/1...d5|'''1...d5''']] is the Scandinavian defence. Black intends to eliminate White's centre, even if they have to give up hopes of a big centre of their own. It is less popular in tournament play than as a club-level reply, where part of its appeal is that it is very forcing and confrontational. White's response is almost always 2. exd5, then Black can recover the pawn if they so choose. [[/1...f5|'''1...f5?''']] is the risky Duras gambit. 2. exf5 and Black has succeeded in deflecting the e pawn away from the centre, but at the cost of a weakened kingside. [[/1...Nf6|'''1...Nf6''']], the Alekhine defence, also attacks e4, but White will rarely allow Black to take it. It usually continues 2. e5 (kicking knight) Nd5 3. d4. Black's real idea is to allow White the centre for now and undermine it later. {{clear}}
{{Chess/board|moves=1. e4 d5|caption=Scandinavian game}} {{Chess/board|moves=1. e4 f5|caption=Duras gambit}} {{Chess/board|moves=1. e4 Nf6|caption=Alekhine defence}}
=== Support d5 === If Black can play a pawn to d5 and keep it there, they get to hold onto a share of the centre. If Black tries to put a pawn on d5 immediately, White will capture it (the Scandinavian defence, above), so in order to maintain a pawn on d5 Black needs to be able to ''recapture'' with a pawn from either c6 or e6. [[/1...e6|'''1...e6''']] is the French defence. After 2. d4 d5, Black keeps a share of the centre, and White's continuations include opening the position with exd5 exd5, or closing the position with e5. The key disadvantage of the French defence is that Black's Queen-side bishop is a liability and hard to develop, because the e6 pawn blocks it in. [[/1...c6|'''1...c6''']] is the Caro-Kann. This has the advantage of avoiding the unhappy ''French bishop'', but instead it takes the c6 square away from Black's knight, and if Black should want to play c5 later, they have lost a tempo. {{clear}}
{{Chess/board|moves=1. e4 e6|caption=French defence}} {{Chess/board|moves=1. e4 c6|caption=Caro-Kann}}
=== Relinquish the centre === {{Chess/board|moves=1. e4 d6 2. d4 Nf6 3. e5 dxe5 4. dxe5 Qxd1+ 5. Kxd1 Ng4|caption=Position in the Pirc defence after '''1. e4 d6 2. d4 Nf6 3. e5 dxe5 4. dxe5 Qxd1+ 5. Kxd1 Ng4!'''|float=right|frame=1}} Black can relinquish control of the centre for now, looking to develop pieces instead. They want to later undermine the centre, and control it from afar with their pieces rather than their pawns. One way Black can control the centre from afar is by fianchettoing a bishop (developing it to the flank, to either g7 or b7, where it controls a long diagonal running through the centre). [[/1...g6|'''1...g6''']] is the Modern defence. Black will follow with Bg7 to control a swathe of the centre before making more committal pawn moves. [[/1...b6|'''1...b6''']] is Owen's defence. Black intends Bb7 to control the other long diagonal. Unlike the Modern, this doesn't have the benefit of preparing kingside castling. [[/1...d6|'''1...d6''']] is the Pirc defence. Black would like to play Nf6 to control the centre. d6 is prophylaxis to prevent White from playing e5: after 1...d6 2. d4 Nf6, the move 3. e5 doesn't work because of 3...dxe5 4. dxe5 Qxd1+ 5. Kxd1 Ng4! forking the pawns on e5 and f2 (contrast [[/1...Nf6|1...Nf6]], the Alekhine defence). Black usually fianchettos the bishop to Bg7 in this opening as well. [[/1...Nc6|'''1...Nc6''']], the Nimzowitsch defence, develops the knight to control d4 and e5. This allows 2. d4, and then Black can choose to respond with either 2...d5 or 2...e5. {{clear}}
{{Chess/board|moves=1. e4 g6|caption=Modern defence}} {{Chess/board|moves=1. e4 b6|caption=Owen's defence}} {{Chess/board|moves=1. e4 d6|caption=Pirc defence}} {{Chess/board|moves=1. e4 Nc6|caption=Nimzowitsch defence}}
=== Rare responses === Other responses include: *[[/1...a6|'''1...a6''']], the St. George defence, mostly famous for having been played by Tony Miles against then World Champion [[wikipedia:Anatoly Karpov|Anatoly Karpov]]. Often Black will follow up with 2...b5, expanding on the queenside. * [[/1...f6|'''1...f6?''']], the Barnes Defence. A rare move that is not a good idea, as it removes the f6 square for the knight and weakens the kingside. Even so, Thomas Wilson Barnes (after whom it is named) beat Paul Morphy, one of the strongest chess players of the 19th century, with this opening. Its only benefit is that it gets out of theory. * [[/1...g5|'''1...g5?''']], the Borg Defence (opposite of Grob) is another option Black has, however it is a bad one because it weakens the kingside severely. The g5-pawn can serve as a hook for White (h4) later on. This also can lead to a Fool's Mate for Black. * [[/1...a5|'''1...a5?!''']], the Ware Defence is equally weak as 1...h5. It just wastes a tempo. * [[/1...h5|'''1...h5?!''']], the Pickering Defence simply wastes a tempo and weakens Black's position. * [[/1...h6|'''1...h6?!''']], the Carr Defence, is another time-wasting move, but it usually transposes into the Borg Defence anyways after 2. d4 g5. * [[/1...Na6|'''1...Na6?!''']] develops the b8-knight to an inferior square. *[[/1...Nh6|'''1...Nh6?!''']] develops the g8-knight to an inferior square. * [[/1...b5|'''1...b5?''']] simply loses a pawn to 2.
